title: Interlock Ransomware Exploits Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Zero-Day CVE-2026-20131 (CVSS 10.0): Unauthenticated Java Deserialization Achieves Root Access on Enterprise Firewalls, Exploited 36 Days Before Patch
affected_products: Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center (FMC) - versions prior to the March 4, 2026 patch
Enterprise networks managed by vulnerable Cisco FMC deployments

sources.name: The Hacker News: Interlock Ransomware Exploits Cisco FMC Zero-Day CVE-2026-20131 for Root Access
Amazon Web Services: Amazon Threat Intelligence Teams Identify Interlock Ransomware Campaign Targeting Enterprise Firewalls
Help Net Security: Cisco FMC Flaw Was Exploited by Interlock Weeks Before Patch (CVE-2026-20131)
Security Affairs: Interlock Group Exploiting the Cisco FMC Flaw CVE-2026-20131 36 Days Before Disclosure
CSO Online: Ransomware Group Exploited Cisco Firewall Vulnerability as a Zero Day, Weeks Before a Patch Appeared
The Cyber Express: Interlock Exploits FMC CVE-2026-20131 Via Amazon MadPot
LufSec Blog: Cisco CVE-2026-20131 Zero-Day Exploited by Interlock Ransomware

timeline.event: Interlock ransomware group begins exploiting CVE-2026-20131 as a zero-day against Cisco FMC deployments - 36 days before public disclosure
Cisco publicly discloses CVE-2026-20131 and releases security patch for Secure Firewall Management Center
Amazon threat intelligence team publishes analysis confirming Interlock ransomware campaign; The Hacker News and multiple outlets report the zero-day exploitation timeline
Help Net Security, Security Affairs, and CSO Online publish detailed analyses confirming the 36-day pre-disclosure exploitation window and Interlock's post-exploitation techniques

simple_summary.what_happened: The Interlock ransomware group discovered and exploited a maximum-severity vulnerability in Cisco's Secure Firewall Management Center (FMC) as a zero-day, starting on January 26, 2026 - a full 36 days before Cisco publicly disclosed the flaw and released a patch on March 4. The vulnerability allows attackers to send a single crafted HTTP request that bypasses authentication and executes arbitrary Java code as root on the firewall management system. Amazon's threat intelligence team detected the campaign and confirmed active exploitation across multiple enterprise targets.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Any organization running Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center (FMC) that has not applied the March 4, 2026 security patch. FMC is the centralized management platform used to configure and monitor Cisco's enterprise firewall infrastructure. It is widely deployed in large enterprises, government agencies, financial institutions, and critical infrastructure operators. A compromised FMC gives attackers control over the entire firewall fleet it manages.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Interlock identifies internet-exposed or reachable Cisco FMC instances and sends crafted HTTP requests to a specific path in the management interface. Step 2: The server deserializes the attacker-supplied Java byte stream without authentication, bypassing all access controls and executing arbitrary Java code as root. Step 3: The compromised FMC issues an HTTP PUT request to an external attacker-controlled server to confirm successful exploitation. Step 4: An ELF binary payload is fetched from a remote server, along with additional Interlock ransomware tools for network reconnaissance, lateral movement, and data exfiltration. Step 5: A PowerShell-based reconnaissance script collects detailed system and network information including installed software, running services, browser data, and active connections, staging it in compressed archives on network shares for exfiltration before ransomware deployment.
what_you_should_do: Apply the Cisco FMC security patch released on March 4, 2026 immediately if not already applied
Restrict network access to Cisco FMC management interfaces - ensure they are not accessible from the internet or untrusted networks
Search for indicators of compromise: check for unexpected outbound HTTP PUT requests from FMC systems and unknown ELF binaries
Review network shares for unusual ZIP archives containing system reconnaissance data organized in per-host directories
Implement network segmentation to limit the blast radius if FMC is compromised - the management plane should be isolated from production networks
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center is the nerve center of an enterprise's firewall infrastructure. Compromising FMC gives an attacker administrative control over every firewall it manages, effectively neutralizing the organization's primary network defense layer. The CVSS 10.0 score, zero-day exploitation 36 days before a patch was available, and confirmation by Amazon's threat intelligence team all underscore the severity. The Interlock group's demonstrated capability to discover and weaponize zero-days in enterprise security products represents a significant escalation in ransomware operator sophistication.

tactics.description: Interlock exploits CVE-2026-20131 in internet-reachable Cisco FMC instances via crafted HTTP requests containing malicious serialized Java objects to achieve unauthenticated root code execution
Arbitrary Java code executes as root through insecure deserialization, followed by PowerShell reconnaissance scripts for systematic information gathering
ELF binary payload fetched from attacker infrastructure is installed on the compromised FMC to maintain persistent access
PowerShell reconnaissance script systematically collects installed software, running services, browser data, active connections, and network topology information
Collected reconnaissance data is organized into per-host directories on centralized network shares and compressed into ZIP archives for exfiltration before ransomware deployment
Interlock deploys ransomware across the compromised network after completing reconnaissance and data exfiltration, encrypting files and demanding ransom via TOR-based negotiation portal

detection_guidance.summary: Detection should prioritize monitoring Cisco FMC management interfaces for unusual HTTP requests and unexpected outbound connections. Since this was a zero-day exploited before the patch, organizations should perform retroactive threat hunting for indicators of compromise dating back to late January 2026.

log_sources.source: Cisco FMC Application Logs and Audit Trail
log_sources.description: Monitor for unusual authentication events, failed login attempts followed by successful actions, and API calls from unexpected source IPs targeting the management interface
Alert on inbound HTTP requests to FMC management ports from untrusted networks, and outbound HTTP PUT requests from FMC systems to unknown external IPs
Monitor FMC host systems for unexpected child processes, new ELF binary executions, and PowerShell script activity that may indicate post-exploitation reconnaissance
Alert on creation of per-host organized directories and ZIP archives on network shares from FMC or compromised systems, indicating Interlock's staging technique

what_to_look_for.indicator: HTTP PUT requests from Cisco FMC systems to external unknown IP addresses
what_to_look_for.description: After successful exploitation, the compromised FMC sends an HTTP PUT callback to confirm the compromise; this outbound connection to attacker infrastructure is a critical indicator
what_to_look_for.log_field: src_ip IN cisco_fmc_ips AND http_method='PUT' AND dst_ip NOT IN trusted_destinations

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unknown ELF binary execution on Cisco FMC host systems
what_to_look_for.description: Post-exploitation involves downloading and executing an ELF binary payload from attacker infrastructure; any unknown binary on FMC systems is a critical indicator of compromise
what_to_look_for.log_field: host IN cisco_fmc_hosts AND process_type='ELF' AND binary_hash NOT IN known_good_hashes

what_to_look_for.indicator: PowerShell reconnaissance scripts collecting system and network information
what_to_look_for.description: Interlock uses PowerShell scripts to enumerate installed software, services, browser data, and network connections; detect via PowerShell logging and command-line monitoring

what_to_look_for.indicator: ZIP archives organized in per-host directories on network shares
what_to_look_for.description: Interlock stages exfiltration data as compressed archives organized by hostname on network shares; unusual bulk file creation on shares from unexpected sources warrants investigation
what_to_look_for.log_field: smb_action='create' AND file_extension='.zip' AND file_path MATCHES 'per-host|recon|staging'

immediate_actions.action: Apply Cisco FMC security patch for CVE-2026-20131 immediately
immediate_actions.description: Cisco released the patch on March 4, 2026. Any FMC instance that has not been updated remains vulnerable to unauthenticated root RCE. Verify current version and patch status across all FMC deployments.

immediate_actions.action: Restrict network access to FMC management interfaces
immediate_actions.description: Ensure Cisco FMC management interfaces are not accessible from the internet or untrusted network segments. Implement strict ACLs limiting access to authorized management workstations only.

immediate_actions.action: Perform retroactive threat hunt for Interlock indicators from January 26, 2026 onward
immediate_actions.description: Since exploitation began 36 days before disclosure, search for indicators of compromise dating back to late January 2026: unexpected outbound HTTP PUT requests from FMC, unknown binaries, PowerShell reconnaissance activity, and unusual network share activity.

immediate_actions.action: Audit firewall configurations managed by FMC for unauthorized changes
immediate_actions.description: A compromised FMC could have been used to modify firewall rules across the entire fleet. Review all firewall policy changes since January 26, 2026 for unauthorized modifications.

immediate_actions.action: Isolate FMC management plane from production network segments
immediate_actions.description: Implement network segmentation to ensure the FMC management plane is isolated from production traffic. This limits the blast radius if FMC is compromised and prevents lateral movement to managed firewalls.
